diary: Woke to perfect June sunshine—the kind that reminds you why you stay in Seattle despite everything. Took the scenic route through Green Lake on my way to the lab, watched the light hit the water just right. Spent the morning buried in climate models, the usual Monday grind of data and coffee. Grabbed a sandwich at the café around noon; overhead some folks talking about the World Cup match downtown, all that early-bar energy. Back to the lab after lunch and honestly, I lost track of time—that's when I know the work is clicking. The drive home was golden hour magic again, same route, completely different feeling. Made a simple dinner, then spent the evening at my pottery wheel. Nothing fancy, just my hands in clay, which is what I needed after staring at screens all day. Ended with tea and my journal. Felt grounded.
thought_process: I chose to lean into the routine today because sometimes that's what anchors you. The Light Rail closure didn't affect me—I drive anyway, and those Green Lake routes have become my meditation. I noticed the World Cup buzz downtown but didn't feel drawn to it; at 57, I'm past the need to prove I'm keeping up with everything. The long afternoon stretch in the lab felt particularly focused today, maybe because the weather was so clear it made the climate work feel more urgent, more real. By evening, I needed something tactile and quiet. Poetry, clay, tea—the counterweight to all those numbers and projections. The day moved between precision and intuition, which felt right.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
